desktop_simple.title: Simple Desktop
desktop_simple.summary: You want a computer for everyday tasks — browsing, email, documents, and light media.
desktop_simple.detail: A simple desktop setup prioritizes ease of use and reliability. It should work out of the box, with a familiar interface, automatic updates, and minimal maintenance. Distros in this category ship with a web browser, office suite, media player, and file manager pre-installed.

desktop_polished.title: Polished Desktop
desktop_polished.summary: You value visual design, smooth animations, and a premium feel in your desktop experience.
desktop_polished.detail: A polished desktop goes beyond functional — it should look and feel modern, with attention to typography, animations, blur effects, and consistent theming. These distros invest heavily in a curated user experience, often rivaling macOS or Windows in visual quality.
good_for: Users coming from macOS who value aesthetics
Professionals who want a beautiful work environment
Daily use where you spend hours at the screen
look_for: Modern GNOME, KDE, or custom DEs

desktop_light.title: Lightweight Desktop
desktop_light.summary: You need an OS that runs well on older or resource-constrained hardware.
desktop_light.detail: Lightweight distros are designed to breathe new life into aging hardware. They use minimal desktop environments (Xfce, LXQt, IceWM) that run on as little as 512 MB to 2 GB of RAM, while still providing a usable graphical interface for basic tasks.

dev_general.title: General Development
dev_general.summary: You program web apps, mobile apps, or general-purpose software.
dev_general.detail: A development workstation needs fresh compiler and interpreter versions, good IDE support, containerization tools (Docker, Podman), and access to a large package repository. Rolling or semi-rolling distros are popular here because they provide up-to-date toolchains.
look_for: Flatpak or Snap for IDE sandboxing

dev_ops.title: DevOps / Cloud
dev_ops.summary: You work with containers, CI/CD pipelines, infrastructure-as-code, and cloud deployments.
dev_ops.detail: DevOps workflows require reproducible environments, strong container tooling, and often declarative system configuration. NixOS excels here with its fully reproducible builds. Fedora and openSUSE offer fresh toolchains with enterprise-grade stability.

dev_systems.title: Systems Development
dev_systems.summary: You work on kernels, compilers, embedded firmware, or low-level systems programming.
dev_systems.detail: Systems programming requires access to kernel headers, cross-compilation toolchains, debugging tools (gdb, perf, strace), and often the ability to build and test custom kernels. Source-based or rolling distros work well because they provide the latest development headers.
look_for: Access to kernel source and headers

gaming_easy.title: Easy Gaming
gaming_easy.summary: You want to install Steam and play games with minimal setup.
gaming_easy.detail: Easy gaming distros come with GPU drivers pre-configured, Steam pre-installed or one-click-installable, and Proton/Wine ready for Windows game compatibility. The goal is zero-friction: plug in a controller, launch Steam, and play.
good_for: Controller support out of the box

gaming_performance.title: Performance Gaming
gaming_performance.summary: You want maximum FPS and are willing to tweak kernel schedulers, GPU settings, and system optimizations.
gaming_performance.detail: Performance gaming distros ship with custom kernels (zen, cachyos, TKG), CPU schedulers (BORE, EEVDF), and GPU driver optimizations. They often include tools like MangoHud, gamemode, and custom compositors to minimize latency and maximize frame rates.

gaming_console.title: Console-Style Gaming
gaming_console.summary: You want a couch/TV gaming experience like a Steam Deck or game console.
gaming_console.detail: Console-style distros boot directly into a big-picture gaming interface, optimized for controllers and TV displays. SteamOS and Bazzite provide this experience with immutable, unbreakable bases that update atomically.

server_enterprise.title: Enterprise Server
server_enterprise.summary: You need a server with 10+ year support, certifications, and commercial backing.
server_enterprise.detail: Enterprise servers run mission-critical workloads where stability, security patches, and long-term support are non-negotiable. RHEL, Rocky Linux, and AlmaLinux provide 10-year support cycles with backported security fixes and no disruptive feature changes.

server_home.title: Home Server
server_home.summary: You want to self-host services like media, file sharing, backups, or home automation.
server_home.detail: A home server runs 24/7 with minimal maintenance. It should be easy to install Docker containers, set up file shares (SMB/NFS), run media servers (Plex, Jellyfin), and manage backups. Debian and Ubuntu Server are popular choices for their stability and documentation.

server_containers.title: Containers & Microservices
server_containers.summary: You run containerized workloads with Docker, Podman, or Kubernetes.
server_containers.detail: Container-focused platforms need a minimal, secure host OS that runs container runtimes efficiently. Alpine Linux is the de facto standard for Docker container images due to its tiny size. For the host OS, Fedora CoreOS, Debian, and Ubuntu Server are popular choices.

nas_storage.title: NAS & Storage
nas_storage.summary: You want a network-attached storage appliance for file sharing and data management.
nas_storage.detail: NAS distros provide a web interface for managing storage pools, RAID arrays, file shares, and backups. TrueNAS SCALE offers enterprise ZFS support, while OpenMediaVault is simpler and runs well on Raspberry Pi hardware.

network_appliance.title: Network / Firewall
network_appliance.summary: You need a router, firewall, or custom network appliance OS.
network_appliance.detail: Network appliance distros like OpenWrt replace the firmware on consumer routers with a full Linux stack, enabling advanced routing, firewalling, VPN servers, QoS, and network monitoring. They run on minimal hardware.

media_production.title: Media Production
media_production.summary: You create audio, video, or visual content and need a low-latency, creative-tool-ready OS.
media_production.detail: Media production distros ship with real-time or low-latency kernels for audio work (JACK, PipeWire), video editing (Kdenlive, DaVinci Resolve), and visual arts (GIMP, Blender, Inkscape). They optimize for consistent performance under heavy loads.

security_offensive.title: Pentesting
security_offensive.summary: You perform penetration testing, security auditing, or vulnerability research.
security_offensive.detail: Offensive security distros come pre-loaded with hundreds of tools for network scanning, exploitation, password cracking, wireless attacks, and forensics. Kali Linux and Parrot OS are the industry standards, used by security professionals and in certifications.

security_privacy.title: Privacy & Anonymity
security_privacy.summary: You need an OS designed to protect your identity and communications.
security_privacy.detail: Privacy-focused distros route all traffic through Tor, use encrypted storage, and minimize data persistence. Tails is amnesic (erases everything on shutdown), while Whonix uses VM isolation to prevent IP leaks. Qubes OS compartmentalizes everything in separate VMs.

education.title: Education
education.summary: You need an OS for classroom use with educational software for students.
education.detail: Education-focused distros ship with age-appropriate software, classroom management tools, and parental controls. They're designed for school labs, young learners, and educational institutions that need a managed, safe computing environment.

learning.title: Learning Linux
learning.summary: You want to deeply understand how Linux works by building and configuring from scratch.
learning.detail: Learning distros force you to understand each component of the system — the boot process, init system, package management, kernel, and shell. Arch Linux, Gentoo, and Slackware are popular choices because they require hands-on configuration of every layer.

immutable_desktop.title: Immutable Desktop
immutable_desktop.summary: You want an unbreakable desktop with atomic updates and guaranteed rollback.
immutable_desktop.detail: Immutable desktops use read-only root filesystems where the OS image is updated atomically. If an update breaks something, you can instantly roll back to the previous state. Apps run in Flatpak or container isolation. Fedora Silverblue and Vanilla OS pioneer this approach.

beginner.title: Beginner
beginner.summary: You've never used Linux and have no command-line experience.
beginner.detail: As a beginner, you need a distro that works entirely through graphical interfaces. Everything — installing apps, managing files, configuring Wi-Fi, printing — should be doable without ever opening a terminal.

newcomer.title: Newcomer
newcomer.summary: You have basic tech skills and maybe some command-line exposure.
newcomer.detail: As a newcomer, you're comfortable following step-by-step guides and aren't afraid of the terminal, but you don't want to rely on it for daily tasks. A good newcomer distro provides GUIs for common tasks while teaching you terminal skills gradually.

intermediate.title: Intermediate
intermediate.summary: You can follow guides and use the terminal for basic tasks.
intermediate.detail: At the intermediate level, you can install packages from the command line, edit config files, and troubleshoot basic issues using search engines and forums. You want a distro that respects your growing skills without overwhelming you.

competent.title: Competent
competent.summary: You're comfortable with package managers, services, and system configuration.
competent.detail: Competent users can manage systemd services, configure network settings, set up SSH servers, and debug most issues independently. You want a distro that gives you more control and doesn't hide complexity behind abstraction layers.
look_for: Access to bleeding-edge or rolling repos

advanced.title: Advanced
advanced.summary: You configure complex setups, manage services, and compile software from source.
advanced.detail: Advanced users have installed Arch, managed servers, written shell scripts, and compiled custom kernels. You want maximum control and minimal hand-holding. DIY distros like Arch, Void, and NixOS give you the building blocks.

expert.title: Expert
expert.summary: You've built Linux From Scratch, patched kernels, or built custom distros.
expert.detail: At the expert level, you understand the full Linux stack from bootloader to userspace. Gentoo's USE flags, NixOS's declarative model, and Slackware's simplicity appeal to you. You choose distros as tools, not as environments.
look_for: Control over every package and dependency

pragmatist.title: Pragmatist
pragmatist.summary: You use whatever works best — open source or proprietary.
pragmatist.detail: Pragmatists don't have ideological preferences about software licenses. If NVIDIA's proprietary driver gives better performance, use it. If Chrome works better than Firefox for your workflow, use it. The tool serves the task.

moderate.title: Moderate
moderate.summary: You lean toward open source but use proprietary software when it's clearly better.
moderate.detail: Moderates prefer FOSS defaults but don't avoid proprietary tools when they solve real problems. You might use Firefox over Chrome, LibreOffice over MS Office, but still install Steam or use NVIDIA drivers without guilt.

soft_libre.title: Soft Libre
soft_libre.summary: You seek out open-source alternatives and choose the FOSS option when reasonably possible.
soft_libre.detail: Soft Libre users actively research FOSS alternatives before reaching for proprietary tools. You'd use GIMP instead of Photoshop, KDE instead of macOS, and PipeWire instead of proprietary audio. But you're not dogmatic about it.

libre_leaning.title: Libre-Leaning
libre_leaning.summary: You try to use FOSS whenever possible and actively avoid proprietary tools.
libre_leaning.detail: Libre-leaning users make deliberate choices to minimize proprietary dependencies. You might use a different graphics card to avoid NVIDIA's proprietary driver, or choose Debian's main repo over contrib/non-free.

foss_advocate.title: FOSS Advocate
foss_advocate.summary: You advocate for software freedom and contribute to open-source projects.
foss_advocate.detail: FOSS advocates see software freedom as a social good. You contribute to open-source projects, prefer copyleft licenses, and choose distros that align with these values. Fedora, Debian, and openSUSE reflect this ethos.

foss_purist.title: FOSS Purist
foss_purist.summary: You refuse all proprietary software, including firmware and drivers.
foss_purist.detail: FOSS purists follow the Free Software Foundation's standards. Only fully libre distros like Trisquel (FSF-endorsed) qualify. This means no proprietary firmware, no binary blobs, and potentially reduced hardware support — a trade-off for absolute software freedom.

systemd_free.title: Systemd-Free
systemd_free.summary: You specifically want to avoid systemd as your init system.
systemd_free.detail: Some users dislike systemd's scope (it handles init, logging, networking, DNS, timers, and more). Alternatives include OpenRC (Alpine, Artix, Gentoo), runit (Void Linux, Artix), s6, and dinit. Devuan offers the Debian ecosystem without systemd.
look_for: OpenRC, runit, s6, or dinit init system

preconfigured.title: Preconfigured
preconfigured.summary: Everything is set up out of the box — sensible defaults, no tweaking.
preconfigured.detail: Preconfigured distros ship with well-chosen defaults so you can be productive immediately. The desktop, apps, themes, and settings are ready to use. You trade customization depth for immediate usability.

curated.title: Curated
curated.summary: A thoughtfully designed experience — opinionated choices that produce a polished result.
curated.detail: Curated distros like elementary OS and Zorin OS make strong design decisions to create a cohesive experience. Instead of offering every option, they choose the best option and polish it. Think Apple's approach applied to Linux.

moderate_custom.title: Moderate Customization
moderate_custom.summary: Settings panels, config file tweaks, and theme changes — you like adjusting things.
moderate_custom.detail: Moderate customizers use GUI settings panels and occasionally edit config files. KDE Plasma is ideal here: thousands of settings, but all accessible through graphical tools. You can change workflows without touching the terminal.

tweaker.title: Tweaker
tweaker.summary: You want to modify kernel parameters, manage services, and tune everything to perfection.
tweaker.detail: Tweakers go beyond settings panels into sysctl parameters, systemd unit files, filesystem tuning, and boot configuration. Arch and openSUSE Tumbleweed provide the rolling packages and deep access this requires.
look_for: AUR or OBS for custom packages

diy.title: DIY
diy.summary: You want to choose every component and assemble your system from a minimal base.
diy.detail: DIY distros give you a minimal base (just a kernel, shell, and package manager) and let you build up. You choose the init system, display server, window manager, shell, and every application. Arch Linux is the gold standard.

from_scratch.title: From Scratch
from_scratch.summary: You compile the entire system from source code, optimized for your specific hardware.
from_scratch.detail: Source-based distros like Gentoo compile every package from source with USE flags that let you include or exclude specific features. This produces a system perfectly tailored to your hardware, but requires significant compilation time.

declarative.title: Declarative
declarative.summary: You define your entire system in a configuration file — fully reproducible.
declarative.detail: NixOS pioneered the declarative approach: your entire system (packages, services, users, networking) is described in a single configuration.nix file. Any machine with this file produces an identical system. This is revolutionary for DevOps and reproducibility.

immutable.title: Immutable / Atomic
immutable.summary: A locked-down root filesystem with automatic, rollback-capable updates.
immutable.detail: Immutable systems use read-only root partitions updated atomically. SteamOS, Fedora Silverblue, Bazzite, and Vanilla OS use this model. Apps install via Flatpak or containers. The system itself cannot be accidentally broken.

modern_nvidia.title: Modern (NVIDIA)
modern_nvidia.summary: A modern PC with a discrete NVIDIA graphics card.
modern_nvidia.detail: NVIDIA on Linux requires proprietary drivers for best performance. Some distros (Pop!_OS, Manjaro, Garuda) ship with NVIDIA drivers pre-installed. Others (Fedora) require manual setup via RPMFusion. The experience varies significantly across distros.

modern_standard.title: Modern (AMD/Intel)
modern_standard.summary: A modern PC with AMD or Intel graphics (integrated or discrete).
modern_standard.detail: AMD and Intel GPUs use open-source Mesa drivers built into the kernel. They just work on virtually every Linux distro without any proprietary driver installation. This is the easiest hardware path to Linux.
look_for: Latest kernel for newest GPU support

old_moderate.title: Older Hardware
old_moderate.summary: A PC from pre-2015 or with 2-4 GB RAM.
old_moderate.detail: Older hardware needs lightweight distros that don't demand modern GPU features or lots of RAM. Xfce, LXQt, and MATE desktops work well. Distros like MX Linux, Xubuntu, and antiX are specifically designed for this use case.

ultra_light.title: Ultra-Lightweight
ultra_light.summary: Very old hardware with less than 2 GB RAM or an ancient CPU.
ultra_light.detail: Ultra-lightweight distros like Puppy Linux, antiX, and Tiny Core can run on machines with 256 MB to 1 GB RAM. They often load entirely into RAM, use window managers instead of desktop environments, and sacrifice modern features for speed.

apple_silicon.title: Apple Silicon
apple_silicon.summary: An Apple Mac with M1, M2, M3, or M4 chip.
apple_silicon.detail: Apple Silicon Macs require the Asahi Linux project's reverse-engineered drivers. Support has matured significantly — GPU acceleration, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and audio all work. Based on Fedora, it's currently the only way to run Linux natively on modern Macs.

arm.title: ARM / SBC
arm.summary: Raspberry Pi or other ARM-based single-board computer.
arm.detail: ARM SBCs (Raspberry Pi, Pine64, ODROID) need distros compiled for ARM architecture. Raspberry Pi OS is the standard for Pis. Many distros (Debian, Ubuntu, Fedora) also offer ARM images. Package availability may be more limited than x86.
look_for: SD card and eMMC boot support

virtual.title: Virtual / Cloud
virtual.summary: Running inside a virtual machine or cloud instance.
virtual.detail: VMs and cloud instances don't need hardware drivers — they use paravirtualized devices. Focus on minimal footprint, fast boot, and good cloud-init support. Alpine, Debian, and Ubuntu are the most popular choices for cloud deployments.

bleeding_edge.title: Bleeding Edge
bleeding_edge.summary: Always the newest versions — you accept the risk of occasional breakage.
bleeding_edge.detail: Bleeding-edge distros push updates as soon as upstream projects release them, often with minimal testing. You get the latest features and security fixes immediately, but updates can occasionally break things. Arch Linux and its derivatives are the archetype.

rolling_tested.title: Rolling (Tested)
rolling_tested.summary: Continuous updates, but held back a few days/weeks for testing.
rolling_tested.detail: Tested rolling distros delay updates for quality assurance. Manjaro holds Arch packages for 1-2 weeks. openSUSE Tumbleweed runs every snapshot through openQA automated testing. You get fresh software with fewer surprises.

regular_release.title: Regular Release
regular_release.summary: New versions every 6-12 months, with upgrade paths between releases.
regular_release.detail: Fixed-release distros (Fedora, Ubuntu) ship a new version every 6-12 months. Each release is a tested snapshot. You upgrade when ready, getting new features and packages in batches rather than continuously.

lts.title: Long-Term Support
lts.summary: Security patches for 3-5 years on a stable base — no feature churn.
lts.detail: LTS releases receive security-only updates with no risky feature changes. Ubuntu LTS (5 years), Debian Stable (3 years + 2 LTS), and Linux Mint follow this model. Ideal for workstations and servers that need reliability over freshness.

set_and_forget.title: Set & Forget
set_and_forget.summary: Install once, apply auto-updates, never think about it again.
set_and_forget.detail: Set-and-forget systems need automatic unattended updates, robust error recovery, and minimal maintenance. NAS platforms (TrueNAS, OpenMediaVault) and immutable desktops (Silverblue, Bazzite) excel here because they update atomically.

enterprise_stable.title: Enterprise Stable
enterprise_stable.summary: 10-year certified support with backported security fixes. Rock-solid reliability.
enterprise_stable.detail: Enterprise distros (RHEL, Rocky, Alma) provide a decade of support with rigorous testing, CVE patching, and ABI stability. They're certified for enterprise hardware and software. Packages are older but proven in production.

de_full_modern.title: Full Modern DE
de_full_modern.summary: GNOME-style desktop with activities overview, gestures, and Wayland.
de_full_modern.detail: Modern DEs (GNOME, Pantheon, Budgie) use activity-based workflows — an overview of all windows, virtual desktops, and app search. They prioritize clean design, Wayland support, touchpad gestures, and minimal visual clutter. Similar to macOS in philosophy.

de_full_traditional.title: Full Traditional DE
de_full_traditional.summary: Windows-like desktop with taskbar, start menu, and system tray.
de_full_traditional.detail: Traditional DEs (KDE Plasma, Cinnamon, MATE) use a panel-at-bottom layout with a start menu, taskbar/dock, system tray, and desktop icons. Familiar to Windows users. KDE Plasma is the most customizable DE in Linux.

de_lightweight.title: Lightweight DE
de_lightweight.summary: A functional desktop that uses minimal RAM — Xfce, LXQt, or MATE.
de_lightweight.detail: Lightweight DEs provide a full desktop experience (panels, file manager, settings) while using 300-600 MB RAM idle. Xfce is the most popular, LXQt is the lightest with a Qt-based interface, and MATE continues the GNOME 2 legacy.

de_tiling.title: Tiling WM
de_tiling.summary: Keyboard-driven window management — windows automatically tile without overlapping.
de_tiling.detail: Tiling WMs (Hyprland, Sway, i3, dwm) arrange windows in non-overlapping tiles across virtual desktops. Navigation is entirely keyboard-driven. Once mastered, they're extremely productive for code-heavy workflows. Steep learning curve.

de_minimal.title: Minimal WM
de_minimal.summary: A basic floating window manager — you build the rest of the environment yourself.
de_minimal.detail: Minimal WMs (Openbox, IceWM, Fluxbox) provide window drawing and basic management. There's no panel, no file manager, no settings app by default — you add each component yourself. Ideal for learning or extreme customization.

de_none.title: No GUI (Headless)
de_none.summary: Terminal only — for servers, containers, and command-line enthusiasts.
de_none.detail: Headless installations have no graphical interface. All management happens via SSH, terminal, or web UI. This is standard for servers, containers, NAS appliances, and network devices. Saves all resources for the actual workload.
look_for: tmux or screen for session management
